response:
The sequence of pages read each day starts with five, then twelve, then twenty-one.
These numbers suggest a pattern where the difference between terms grows steadily.
The first difference between five and twelve is seven, while the next is nine.
Since the second difference remains constant at two, the sequence is indeed quadratic.
We can express the general term as a polynomial involving the square of n.
Let the formula be defined as P equals an squared plus bn plus c.
Substituting n equals one gives us the equation a plus b plus c equals five.
Substituting n equals two yields foura plus 2b plus c equals twelve.
Substituting n equals three results in ninea plus 3b plus c equals twenty-one.
Solving this system reveals that a equals one, b equals five, and c equals negative one.
Therefore the general formula for pages read on day n is n squared plus five n minus one.
This equation accurately predicts the number of pages for any given day in the challenge.

* * *

To find the total pages read over thirty days we must sum the sequence values.
The sum requires adding the first term through the thirtieth term of the formula.
We can split the summation into three separate parts for easier calculation.
The first part involves summing n squared from one to thirty.
The second part involves summing five times n from one to thirty.
The third part involves summing negative one from one to thirty.
The sum of squares for the first thirty integers equals nine thousand four hundred fifty.
The sum of the first thirty integers equals four hundred fifty.
Multiplying four hundred fifty by five gives us a total of two thousand two hundred fifty.
The sum of negative one thirty times equals negative thirty.
Adding these three results together gives the final total pages read.
Nine thousand four hundred fifty plus two thousand two hundred fifty equals eleven thousand six hundred ninety.
Subtracting thirty from eleven thousand six hundred ninety yields eleven thousand six hundred sixty.
The children will read a total of eleven thousand six hundred sixty pages by the end.
This structured approach ensures significant exposure to literature over the month.
The father successfully uses mathematics to guide his children toward reading more.
Their engagement with books grows as the daily page count increases steadily.
The quadratic nature of the schedule creates a challenging yet achievable goal.
Literature becomes a daily habit through this carefully designed mathematical plan.
